{"text": "--- Page 2 ---\nSlips, the other main type of error, are unintended. A person does something \nthey did not mean to do. Think of a person slipping and falling on a wet floor and \nyou will understand where the term slip comes from. Examples of slips: \n \nYou turn your oven ON to let it heat up before baking something but forget \nto check first to see whether there is anything already in the oven. \n \nYou try to say “she -0.052400533109903336, 0.03776993602514267, -0.005356517620384693, -0.03521532192826271, 0.1014423817396164, 0.02365848422050476, -0.05586038529872894, 0.008136345073580742, -0.04202553257346153, 0.08265611529350281, 0.05275437980890274, -0.007292881142348051, 0.05287950485944748, -0.05430413782596588, -0.0320599339902401]}, "full_text_sample": {"text": "--- Page 1 ---\nMistakes and Slips \n \nPeople make mistakes and commit errors; it’s a fact of life. Nobody is \nperfect. Designers of digital technology have to live with that fact. \nActually, good designers do more than live with it; their designs take it \ninto account. They avoid designs that make it likely for users to make \nerrors (Norman, 2014). They create digital products and services that \nhelp people avoid and recover from errors. \n \nMistakes Versus Slips \nWhen categorizing the types of errors people make, the first distinction is \nbetween mistakes and slips \n \nMistakes are errors of conscious decision. A choice presents itself and a \nperson considers the alternatives, weighs the pros and cons of the alternatives \n… and chooses the wrong one. Don Norman calls mistakes “errors of \nintention”—the person intentionally decides to do something that turns out to be \nincorrect or to have an undesirable result. Examples of mistakes: \n \nYou shop online for a flash memory card for your camera, but the one you \norder turns out to be incompatible with your camera. \n \nYou take the freeway route to a concert, expecting traffic to be OK, but \nencounter a huge traffic jam. \n \nYou vote for someone because you believe the policies they propose will \nhelp you, but after they are elected, you learn that those policies actually \nharm you. \nPeople make mistakes either because they have an incorrect understanding of \nthe choices or because they have inaccurate or incomplete information. Don \nNorman calls this “having a faulty mental model” (Norman, 1983a). Since \nmistakes are the result of intentional choices and actions, we detect them only \nafterward, when their ramifications become apparent. In engineering terms, we \nsay mistakes can be detected only with feedback. \n\n\n--- Page 2 ---\nSlips, the other main type of error, are unintended. A person does something \nthey did not mean to do. Think of a person slipping and falling on a wet floor and \nyou will understand where the term ", "embedding": [-0.03920178487896919, 0.06091057136654854, 0.029535824432969093, 0.004191968124359846, {"text": "--- Page 2 ---\nSlips, the other main type of error, are unintended. A person does something \nthey did not mean to do. Think of a person slipping and falling on a wet floor and \nyou will understand where the term slip comes from. Examples of slips: \n \nYou turn your oven ON to let it heat up before baking something but forget \nto check first to see whether there is anything already in the oven. \n \nYou try to say “she sells seashells by the seashore” quickly, but your \nmouth won’t say the right w...", "embedding": [-0.07095123827457428, -0.03230015188455582, 0.09637980163097382, 0.08097556233406067, -0.052400533109903336, 0.03776993602514267, -0.005356517620384693, -0.03521532192826271, 0.1014423817396164, 0.02365848422050476, -0.05586038529872894, 0.008136345073580742, -0.04202553257346153, 0.08265611529350281, 0.05275437980890274, -0.007292881142348051, 0.05287950485944748, -0.05430413782596588, -0.0320599339902401]}, "full_text_sample": {"text": "--- Page 1 ---\nMistakes and Slips \n \nPeople make mistakes and commit errors; it’s a fact of life. Nobody is \nperfect. Designers of digital technology have to live with that fact. \nActually, good designers do more than live with it; their designs take it \ninto account. They avoid designs that make it likely for users to make \nerrors (Norman, 2014). They create digital products and services that \nhelp people avoid and recover from errors. \n \nMistakes Versus Slips \nWhen categorizing the types of errors people make, the first distinction is \nbetween mistakes and slips \n \nMistakes are errors of conscious decision. A choice presents itself and a \nperson considers the alternatives, weighs the pros and cons of the alternatives \n… and chooses the wrong one. Don Norman calls mistakes “errors of \nintention”—the person intentionally decides to do something that turns out to be \nincorrect or to have an undesirable result. Examples of mistakes: \n \nYou shop online for a flash memory card for your camera, but the one you \norder turns out to be incompatible with your camera. \n \nYou take the freeway route to a concert, expecting traffic to be OK, but \nencounter a huge traffic jam. \n \nYou vote for someone because you believe the policies they propose will \nhelp you, but after they are elected, you learn that those policies actually \nharm you. \nPeople make mistakes either because they have an incorrect understanding of \nthe choices or because they have inaccurate or incomplete information. Don \nNorman calls this “having a faulty mental model” (Norman, 1983a). Since \nmistakes are the result of intentional choices and actions, we detect them only \nafterward, when their ramifications become apparent. In engineering terms, we \nsay mistakes can be detected only with feedback. \n\n\n--- Page 2 ---\nSlips, the other main type of error, are unintended. A person does something \nthey did not mean to do. Think of a person slipping and falling on a wet floor and \nyou will understand where the term ", "embedding": [-0.03920178487896919, 0.06091057136654854, 0.029535824432969093, 0.004191968124359846,
